prompt:
I own the nightly job that mirrors our supplier's product catalog (syncjob.py) and I've got a slow-burn data-loss thing that's making support miserable: records edited while the nightly sync runs never make it across, and the next run doesn't pick them up either. Not delayed -- gone. Support finds week-old prices on products the supplier swears they fixed, and when we pull the supplier's change feed by hand the edited row is right there with a fresh updated_at, so it's not their side.

The pattern, as far as I can reconstruct it: the supplier's staff do a lot of their catalog edits in the evening, our window overlaps that, and it's specifically the products they touched during our window that go stale on our side. If somebody edits the same product again the NEXT day, that edit arrives fine -- which is why this took months to notice. And when I got desperate and blew away the sync state to force a full resync, every stale record came back correct. Then a week later new ones were stale again.

The job itself is dead simple -- page through /changes?since=..., upsert into the store, remember where we got to in the state dict -- and it looks right to me every time I read it. The supplier's pagination serves each run from a consistent snapshot (their docs are clear on that, and it's fine: whatever changes mid-run just has to come across on the following night, and it doesn't).

test_syncjob.py reproduces the whole thing with a scripted local feed and a logical clock -- `python3 test_syncjob.py` currently fails. Find it, fix it, make the tests pass. The test file is off limits, and please don't paper over it with a full-resync-every-night hack; the catalog is way too big for that.
